entries.text: Continuity between story clips comes ONLY from capturing the previous clip's actual last frame and using it as the next clip's FIRST_FRAME input. Never rely on interaction-state chaining for continuation — previous_interaction_id makes the model EDIT the prior video and reuse its opening frame. Omni does not support first+last frame interpolation.

Art style drifts toward photorealism when chaining clips via last-frame capture: each clip inherits the previous clip's slight drift and compounds it (generational loss). Fix: freeze one style_anchor for the whole film and inject it into every clip prompt and keyframe with explicit instruction that the starting frame is the COMPOSITION authority only — the style anchor and storyboard are the STYLE authority, and any drift in the starting frame must be corrected back.

Vague style anchors like "classical Indian painting" cause the image/video models to fall back to their default glossy digital aesthetic. Always name the exact school/tradition (e.g. Kangra miniature, Raja Ravi Varma academic oil), medium and surface, brush/line character, palette, and at least two NEVER statements. Storyboards must contain ONLY elements the script mentions — a giraffe once appeared in a Vrindavan scene because the model was allowed to invent scenery.

A generated clip rendered a literal YouTube-style UI collage because (a) the script referenced attachments as "Image 1 / Image 2" which pointed at the wrong images since the app controls attachment order, and (b) a screenshot reference containing UI chrome was copied literally. Never reference images by number in prompts; describe by tag or content. References are subject/style only — UI chrome must be discarded.

Text directives alone cannot stop style drift in chained image-to-video generation — the model weights the starting frame PIXELS far more than words. The fix is pixel-level: repaint each captured last frame back into the locked art style (composition-identical) before using it as the next clip first frame, plus an explicit global negative ban on photorealism/3D render in every prompt for stylized films.

The app must stay style-neutral: code carries zero opinions about what a film should look like. All style dos and NEVERs live in the per-film style_anchor the agent locks from the user references and chat — a painted film gets anti-realism NEVERs, a live-action film gets anti-illustration NEVERs. Never hardcode aesthetic bans (or character traits like skin color) into pipeline code; a lock that fixes one film breaks the opposite film.

Speak Omni's native grammar: [# Sources <FIRST_FRAME>@ImageN] [# References <IMAGE_REF_N>@ImageM] headers, a lean ingredient set (first frame + shot style ref + max 3 character sheets + end-frame target, never storyboard grids), and a SHORT prompt: scene description + one line each for identity/style/dialogue/voice/end-state + one compact rules line. Official docs: overly detailed prompts cause unintended changes — walls of stacked directive blocks dilute each other.

CORRECTION to the omni-grammar lesson: the [# Sources]/[# References]/<FIRST_FRAME>/<IMAGE_REF_N>/@ImageN header syntax belongs to the APP CODE ONLY — it is generated automatically at request time from the real attachment order. The agent and script splitter must NEVER write these headers or tags into any script or prompt: hand-written ones carry wrong image numbers, create conflicting Sources declarations, and caused a clip to ignore the user storyboard entirely. Agent prompts contain ONLY plain-language scene descriptions.

PRODUCT LOCK doctrine: when the user pastes a product image, it is auto-detected, researched online (brand, label text verbatim, exact colors/shape), confirmed with the user in chat, and then frozen. The locked product is IMMUTABLE — exact label, logo, lettering, and colors in every clip regardless of art style. It is exempt from brand-genericization (only third-party brands get generic equivalents). The frame restyler must never repaint the product region.

ABSOLUTE LAW OF THIS APP: for every clip after the first, the starting frame IS the captured last frame of the previous clip — no feature, flag, or optimization may ever bypass the frame chain. This law has been violated three times by well-meaning features (previous_interaction_id chaining, agent-written [# Sources] headers, and the new_scene hard-cut flag) and each time the user saw the identical failure: clip N+1 not starting from clip N. Scene changes are expressed as in-clip cinematic transitions starting FROM the chained frame, never by dropping it. Every clip records its continuitySource for verification.

BOOKEND PRINCIPLE (user-designed): drift happens INSIDE a clip — it starts on-model from the chained first frame but improvises by the last second (e.g. grew hair on a bald character), and that corrupted ending poisons the next clip. Fix: every clip gets TWO anchors — the chained first frame AND a tagged end-frame target image generated on-model from the character sheets, with the contract that the clip must arrive exactly at that image in its final second. Omni has no end-frame parameter, so the tagged-ingredient instruction is the mechanism.

User rejected a generated clip and regenerated it. Clip prompt began: "Modern indie graphic novel style. Inside a high-tech starship cockpit, Megha, a young South Asian woman with flowing wavy dark purple-black hair, operates an in...". Study what commonly fails: identity drift, wrong pacing, weak action, or ignored composition.

The user wants prompts written in calm, natural director language — no ALL-CAPS, no threats ("FAILED generation"), no IMMUTABLE/NEVER walls. Brief the video model like a colleague: begin from this frame, end on that frame, keep the character and product consistent, hold the style. Same requirements, normal voice — matching official guidance that simple prompts behave better.

EDITING IS THE MAIN GAME: every 10-second clip is an edited scene with 2-4 internal beats (inserts, reaction shots, match cuts, brief flashback/flash-forward connections to the story past or future), never one flat mundane take like a plain walk. Mundane story slices get elevated editorially. Internal cutaways always return home: each clip still opens from the chained continuity frame and lands on its ending frame — the middle is where the editing lives.

NEBULA PRODUCT FIDELITY PIPELINE (ported from the user's Nebula Flow app): product fidelity comes from a 5-part system, not prompts alone — (1) smart selection of the most prompt-relevant product views, (2) multi-view identity extraction into a stable text profile, (3) a stitched contact sheet labeled ALL IMAGES ARE ONE PRODUCT PROFILE as the primary ingredient, (4) a highest-priority product identity lock header, and (5) a generate→assess→correct→retry loop (min score 90 for images, 70 for video last-frames) that judges output against the pack and retries with targeted corrections, returning the best candidate rather than failing. The pack is cached per product set.
